words.definition: To make suffering, deficiency, or a problem less severe.
words.example: He couldn't cure her disease, but tried to alleviate her pain.

words.definition: Having or showing care and conscientiousness in one's work or duties.
words.example: The discovery was made after years of diligent research.

words.definition: Delay or postpone action; put off doing something.
words.example: It is tempting to procrastinate when faced with a difficult task.

words.definition: Able to withstand or recover quickly from difficult conditions.
words.example: Babies are generally far more resilient than new parents realize.

words.definition: Showing great attention to detail; very careful and precise.
words.example: The designs are hand-glazed with meticulous care.

words.definition: Certain to happen; unavoidable.
words.example: War was inevitable.

words.definition: Present, appearing, or found everywhere.
words.example: His ubiquitous influence was felt by all the family.

words.definition: No longer produced or used; out of date.
words.example: The disposal of old and obsolete machinery.

words.definition: Think about (something) carefully.
words.example: I pondered the question of what clothes to wear for the occasion.

words.definition: Unwilling and hesitant; disinclined.
words.example: She seemed reluctant to discuss the matter.

words.definition: Grow or develop well or vigorously.
words.example: Education groups thrive on organization.

words.definition: Susceptible to physical or emotional attack or harm.
words.example: We were in a vulnerable position.

words.definition: Represent (something) as being larger, better, or worse than it really is.
words.example: He was apt to exaggerate any aches and pains.

words.definition: Truthful and straightforward; frank.
words.example: His responses were remarkably candid.

words.definition: Satisfactory or acceptable in quality or quantity.
words.example: This office is perfectly adequate for my needs.

description: Common expressions used in professional environments.

words.definition: Make changes in something established, especially by introducing new methods, ideas, or products.
words.example: The company's failure to innovate caused its downfall.

words.definition: Producing a great deal of profit.
words.example: She has a lucrative career as a stand-up comedian.

words.definition: Dealing with things sensibly and realistically in a way that is based on practical rather than theoretical considerations.
words.example: We need to adopt a pragmatic approach to solve this issue.

words.definition: Work jointly on an activity or project.
words.example: They collaborated on the project to ensure its success.

words.definition: Not or no longer needed or useful; superfluous.
words.example: Many of the old skills had become redundant.

words.definition: Entrust a task or responsibility to another person, typically one who is less senior than oneself.
words.example: He delegates routine tasks to his assistant.

words.definition: Put a decision, plan, agreement, etc. into effect.
words.example: The regulations implement a 1956 treaty.

words.definition: Able to adapt or be adapted to many different functions or activities.
words.example: A versatile sewing machine.

words.definition: Obtain or bring about by discussion.
words.example: He negotiated a new contract with the sellers.

words.definition: Never done or known before.
words.example: The government faces an unprecedented crisis.

words.definition: Possible to do easily or conveniently.
words.example: It is not feasible to put most finds from excavations on public display.

words.definition: A thing that motivates or encourages one to do something.
words.example: Give farmers an incentive to improve their land.

words.definition: A general agreement.
words.example: There is a growing consensus that the current regime has failed.

words.definition: Showing no activity; dull and sluggish.
words.example: A stagnant economy.

words.definition: The state of being legally responsible for something.
words.example: Once you contact the card protection scheme your liability for any loss ends.

description: Words frequently encountered in academic reading and writing.

words.definition: Open to more than one interpretation; not having one obvious meaning.
words.example: The election result was ambiguous.

words.definition: Based on, concerned with, or verifiable by observation or experience rather than theory or pure logic.
words.example: They provided considerable empirical evidence to support their argument.

words.definition: Combine (a number of things) into a coherent whole.
words.example: Students must learn how to synthesize information from various sources.

words.definition: A typical example or pattern of something; a pattern or model.
words.example: There is a new paradigm for public art in this country.

words.definition: Existing in something as a permanent, essential, or characteristic attribute.
words.example: Any form of mountaineering has its inherent dangers.

words.definition: A supposition or proposed explanation made on the basis of limited evidence as a starting point for further investigation.
words.example: His steady state hypothesis of the origin of the universe.

words.definition: Check or prove the validity or accuracy of.
words.example: These clinical trials go a long way to validating the medical claims.

words.definition: Have a mutual relationship or connection.
words.example: The study found that success in the educational system correlates highly with class.

words.definition: Relating to, measuring, or measured by the quality of something rather than its quantity.
words.example: A qualitative change in the undergraduate curriculum.

words.definition: Relating to, measuring, or measured by the quantity of something rather than its quality.
words.example: Quantitative analysis of the data.

words.definition: A person who advocates a theory, proposal, or project.
words.example: A collection of essays by both critics and proponents of graphology.

words.definition: A detailed analysis and assessment of something, especially a literary, philosophical, or political theory.
words.example: A critique of Marxist historicism.

words.definition: A conclusion reached on the basis of evidence and reasoning.
words.example: Researchers are entrusted with drawing inferences from the data.

words.definition: (Of an argument, theory, or policy) logical and consistent.
words.example: They failed to develop a coherent economic strategy.

words.definition: Surround and have or hold within.
words.example: A vast halo encompassing the Milky Way galaxy.
